notes.anchor: nor, in any way, to console you
notes.note: A second letter to the exiled M. Marcellus (cos. 51), in the same campaign of persuasion as Fam. 4.7. Cicero strikes the consolatory pose only to disclaim it: Marcellus's Stoic fortitude needs no comfort, and Cicero confesses he cannot even console himself amid the ruin of the Republic.

notes.anchor: If there is to be any commonwealth
notes.note: The recurring res publica theme of these letters: Cicero argues that whether or not a genuine commonwealth survives under Caesar's dominance, Rome remains the right place for Marcellus to be — either as a leading citizen or, failing that, as the most fitting place even for a kind of internal exile.

notes.anchor: what place is empty of this tyranny?
notes.note: Cicero names Caesar's regime a dominatus (tyranny/sole rule). His point is bleakly pragmatic: since Caesar's power reaches everywhere, fleeing abroad buys no real liberty, so one may as well live at home.

notes.anchor: even the man who holds all things looks kindly upon talent
notes.note: Caesar ("the man who holds all things") is here credited with genuine regard for ability, nobility, and rank — a calculated nod to his clementia, the famous policy of clemency toward pardoned opponents that underlay the recall of Marcellus.
